revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): UNHCR figure corrected to 211,360 (not 211,260).
CORRECTED (Stage 3): RM3B/RM50M estimate comes from 2019 IDEAS study, NOT CGD 2026. Corrected attribution.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 18,000 detention figure is for migrants/refugees/asylum-seekers combined, not refugees alone. Fortify Rights March 2026 reports 21,000+. Revised to 'over 21,000 detained migrants and refugees'.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Employment Act DOES cover undocumented workers per case law (Fice Fransina Nenobais ruling). Removed claim that Employment Act excludes them.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 3D restriction not confirmed as final operative rule in 2026 DPP materials — flagged as announced policy direction, not enacted regulation.

CORRECTED (Stage 3): Thailand 2025 actually PILOTED work permits for Myanmar refugees — removed incorrect claim that Thailand followed 'register first, restrict second' path. Thailand is ahead of Malaysia on this.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): DPP first phase focuses on people already in detention centres, not general population. Corrected scope description.
CORRECTED (Stage 3): Government stated DPP holders would access employment, education, healthcare — broader than just 3D work permits. Acknowledged stated ambition.
ADDED (Stage 2): Acknowledged incremental-progress interpretation and balanced government framing.
ADDED (Stage 2): B40 Malaysian workers' perspective on wage depression and job competition.

ADDED (Stage 2): Religious dimension — political Islam drives selective humanitarianism (Rohingya sympathy from co-religionist solidarity) but reinforces exclusionary policies toward non-Muslim refugees.
ADDED (Stage 4): DPP permits have NOT actually been issued yet as of February 2026. The system is registration-only so far — work permits are announced but not operative.
ADDED (Stage 4): 3,200 children under 12 in detention centres as of December 2025.
ADDED (Stage 4): Sabah has 50,000+ refugees but DPP rollout not begun there due to state objections.
ADDED (Stage 4): Employer incentives to replace undocumented with documented creates market for permits leading to labour broker capture risk.
ADDED (Stage 4): Registration centres only in major cities — rural refugees face travel barriers to register.
ADDED (Stage 4): Indonesia's Presidential Regulation 125/2016 as directly relevant comparator.
ADDED (Stage 4): Jordan compact model and Turkey Temporary Protection as better parallels.
ADDED (Stage 4): Plantation sector exclusion despite US TIP Report flags on forced labour.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): DPP is cynical PR scheme for controlled exploitation, not humanitarian milestone — stated plainly that registration primarily serves the state and employers.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Working-class Bumiputera strongly oppose refugee job expansion — anti-refugee sentiment is mainstream across all races, not fringe.

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Political Islam drives selective humanitarianism — Rohingya get sympathy as fellow Muslims but the same religious politics reinforces exclusionary policies. No party will push for full rights.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Class conflict is the real driver — lower-class workers of ALL races compete with refugees for the same jobs at the same wage level.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): The DPP is likely both the floor and the ceiling for the foreseeable future. No political coalition will expand beyond it.
STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Domestic work inclusion could expose refugee women to Malaysia's most abusive employment sector without enforceable protections.

cards.text: Malaysia launches its first refugee registration system — a genuine acknowledgment that refugees are distinct from illegal immigrants. But work permits have not actually been issued yet, and the announced scope covers only the hardest sectors.
cards.sub: On 1 January 2026, Malaysia launched the Dokumen Pendaftaran Pelarian (DPP), with the first phase targeting people already in detention centres. Over 37,000 individuals screened so far. The government stated DPP holders would access employment, education, and healthcare — but work permits are announced policy, not yet operative as of February 2026. The 3D restriction (manufacturing, mining, construction) is the stated direction. Malaysia has 211,360 UNHCR-registered refugees and is not a signatory to the 1951 Refugee Convention. Thailand in 2025 has already piloted work permits for Myanmar refugees — putting Malaysia behind its neighbour.

cards.h: A 2019 IDEAS study estimated legal refugee employment could add RM3B to GDP — but work permits have not been issued, and when they arrive, B40 Malaysian workers of all races face real wage competition.
cards.s: The RM3B GDP and RM50M tax revenue estimate comes from a 2019 IDEAS study, not a 2026 analysis. The projection depends on assumptions about participation rates and sector allocation that may not hold under a 3D restriction. Employers — represented by the Malaysian Employers Federation — have long sought more documented workers in shortage sectors. Registration creates a market for permits that risks labour broker capture. B40 Malaysian workers across all races in manufacturing, construction, and mining face legitimate wage concerns that macro GDP figures dismiss. The class conflict is real: lower-income workers of all communities compete with refugees for the same jobs at the same wage level.
cards.d: Sources: IDEAS 2019 study, MEF vacancy data and policy submissions, DOSM labour force data. Registration centres are only in major cities — rural refugees face travel barriers. DPP first phase focuses on detention centre populations.

cards.h: Over 21,000 migrants and refugees — including 3,200 children under 12 — sit in immigration detention. Registration alone does not amend the law that holds them.
cards.s: Under the Immigration Act 1959/63, refugees have no legal status distinct from undocumented migrants. Fortify Rights reported 21,000+ in detention as of March 2026, including 3,200 children under 12 as of December 2025. The DPP provides registration but does not change the Act. However, the Employment Act DOES cover undocumented workers per the Fice Fransina Nenobais court ruling — a precedent the analysis initially missed. The DPP's first phase targets those already detained, but amending the Immigration Act requires parliamentary approval that no coalition will seek. Indonesia's Presidential Regulation 125/2016 achieved a more comprehensive framework without Convention signatory status — a directly relevant comparator showing what is possible.
cards.d: Sources: Immigration Act 1959/63, Fortify Rights March 2026, UNHCR Malaysia, Fice Fransina Nenobais ruling, Indonesia Presidential Regulation 125/2016, Bar Council Malaysia analyses.

cards.h: Sabah has 50,000+ refugees but DPP rollout has not begun there. Political Islam drives selective humanitarianism — Rohingya get sympathy as co-religionists while non-Muslim refugees face exclusion.
cards.s: Sabah hosts 50,000+ refugees with completely different dynamics from Peninsular Malaysia, but DPP rollout faces state objections. Sarawak's distinct immigration powers under MA63 raise constitutional questions about applicability. The religious dimension shapes policy: Rohingya receive sympathy as fellow Muslims, but the same religious politics reinforces exclusionary frameworks for non-Muslim refugees (Afghan minorities, Myanmar Christians). Domestic work and plantations — flagged by the US TIP Report for forced labour — are excluded from DPP categories. Including domestic work without enforceable protections could expose refugee women to Malaysia's most abusive employment sector. Anti-refugee sentiment is mainstream across all races, driven by genuine economic anxiety, not just prejudice.
cards.d: Sources: Sabah state migration data, UNHCR Sabah, US TIP Report, ILO domestic worker exploitation research, MA63 immigration provisions, social media monitoring of anti-refugee sentiment.

cards.h: Registration serves the state and employers first. Work permits have not been issued. The DPP is likely both the floor and the ceiling — no coalition will expand beyond it.
cards.text: The state gets a database of previously invisible people it can track and manage. Employers get the prospect of documented sector-locked labour. Refugees get reduced — not eliminated — arrest risk. But permits are not yet operative and the 3D restriction is announced direction, not enacted regulation. Working-class Malaysians of all races oppose expansion because they compete for the same jobs. Political Islam provides selective sympathy for Muslim refugees but reinforces exclusion for others. No party will champion full rights because there is no electoral reward. The DPP is the political maximum. The honest question is not whether Malaysia will build above this floor but whether the system can deliver real protection within constraints that are politically immovable.

The DPP is genuine progress — Malaysia's first formal acknowledgment that refugees are a category distinct from illegal immigrants. Before this, they had zero legal status. That baseline matters. But as of early 2026, work permits remain announced but unissued. The first phase focuses on detention centre populations, where 3,200 children under 12 are held. Thailand has moved ahead by piloting actual work permits for Myanmar refugees. The economic gains projected from a 2019 study depend on assumptions that may not hold under 3D restrictions. Registration without operative permits risks creating a database that serves state surveillance and employer recruitment more than refugee protection. Sabah — home to 50,000+ refugees facing state-level objections — will determine whether the DPP is a national policy or a Peninsular one. The class dimension is inescapable: lower-income Malaysians of all races compete with refugees, and no political force will prioritise refugee rights over citizen anxiety. The DPP is the political maximum Malaysian democracy can currently produce. The task is to ensure it becomes a functioning protection instrument rather than a registration exercise.
